channel.title: Jason Balderaz' sports blog
channel.description: A website about sports and data

item.title: Will the Mountain West Conference survive?
item.dc:creator: Jason Balderaz
item.description:
On September 12, 2024, news broke that five Mountain West Conference teams were leaving the conference to join the new iteration of the Pac-12. It was to be expected as the Pac-12 was looking to rebuild after 10 teams had left the previous summer over media rights disputes. Their first natural target was teams from the Mountain West because they had just reached a deal for teams, Oregon State and Washington State, to fill their schedule against those teams.

It’s the latest development in the ever-changing conference realignment era of college football. Teams move to different places in hopes of a better opportunity for success, NIL fund and reputation. The current era of the Mountain West began in 1999, when eight members of the Western Athletic Conference met at Denver International Airport to agree on terms for what would become the Mountain West Conference, with the hope of pursuing expansion.

Since then, teams like Utah, BYU and TCU have joined and since left the conference, marking the conference’s history with realignment. San Jose State, Boise State, Utah State and Hawai’i joined the conference in the 2010’s, solidifying the current iteration of the Mountain West. Now the conference faces realignment again as Colorado State, San Diego State, Utah State, Fresno State and Boise State are set to join the Pac-12 starting the 2026-27 college football season.

This marks a major concern for the Mountain West, as they are losing valued programs and storied rivalries are now in jeopardy. Let’s take a look at how the departing teams have performed during their tenure in the Mountain West since the conference’s beginning. To do this, we compare overall win percentage to see how they have performed based on their strength of schedule and average win margin (Simple Rating System).

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(gt)

alltime <- read_csv("alltimemwc.csv")

departing <- c("Colorado State", "Boise State", "Utah State", "San Diego State", "Fresno State")

departingteams <- alltime |> filter(School %in% departing)

winpct <- alltime |>
  group_by(School, OPct) |> 
  summarise(
    winpct = OPct,
    srs = SRS)

ggplot() + geom_point(data=winpct, aes(x=OPct, y=srs), color="lightgrey") +
  geom_point(data=departingteams, aes(x=OPct, y=SRS), color="#4F2D7F") +
  geom_text_repel(
    data=departingteams, 
    aes(x=OPct, y=SRS, label=School, hjust = 1.5)) +
  labs(
    x="Win Percentage",
    y="SRS",
    title="How teams have performed all-time", 
    subtitle="The value of the five departing Mountain West teams is undeniable, and the league will suffer for it. Data is since 1999.", 
    caption="Source: Sports-Reference.com | By Jason Balderaz"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=9), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    )

Since the teams in the Mountain West are clearly underperforming compared to power conference teams, no team has ever won a national championship or really been competitive to win one. They stay competitive in Group of 6 play. bowl game appearances, conference championships and power conference wins are valuable to the conference. And when a team like Boise State, who was just in the College Football Playoff two years ago and had a Heisman runner-up, the level of competitiveness dwindles dramatically for the Mountain West.

Even though Boise State has clearly been the powerhouse of the conference, the Air Force Falcons actually lead the league in postseason appearances and wins. They had been to 15 bowls since 1999 and have won nine of those contests, compared to Boise State going to 12 and winning only six of those. Which is somewhat good news for the Mountain West, as they keep a historically decent program in Air Force.

Code 
bowlwins <- alltime |> 
  group_by(School) |> 
  summarise(
    bowlw = sum(BW), 
    bowlapp = sum(BG)) 

ggplot() + 
  geom_segment(
    data=bowlwins, 
    aes(
      y=School, yend=School,
      x=bowlw, xend=bowlapp),
    color="lightgray",
    linewidth = 1.5,
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=bowlwins,
    aes(
      y=School,
      x=bowlw,
      color="Bowl Wins"
    ),
    size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=bowlwins,
    aes(
      y=School,
      x=bowlapp,
    color="Bowl Appearances"
    ),
    size=2
  ) +
 scale_color_manual(name="Bowl Wins", values=c("#4F2D7F","darkgray")) +
    labs(
    x="Bowl Appearances",
    title="The value of the postseason", 
    subtitle="This is ratio of bowl appearnces and wins of each Mountain West team since 1999.", 
    caption="Source: Sports-Reference.com | By Jason Balderaz"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=9), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    )

Shortly after the news broke that those five teams were leaving, the MWC quickly sprang into action to do some damage control. The seven remaining schools signed a memorandum of understanding to remain with the Mountain West. The league was now sitting at a net loss of five teams, so the natural next move was to look toward expansion. On December 18, 2025, Northern Illinois University and the University of Texas at El Paso agreed to join the conference. The move was met with mixed emotions as many were still pulling for a merger between the Pac-12 and the MWC, but it seemed neither side wanted to be the little brother in the situation.

Then, on February 9, 2026, North Dakota State agreed to join the Mountain West as the newest FBS team. This move sent shockwaves through the college football landscape and NDSU is regarded as the greatest FCS team of all time and many were pulling for their jump into the FBS. Other schools such as UC Davis and Grand Canyon University, have also agreed to join the program as non-football members. Now the conference has 10 football members, with a net loss of only two, but the question to be asked is, is it enough?

Code 
allteams25 <- read_csv("teams25.csv")

allteams25 |>
  gt() |> 
    cols_label(
    OPct = "PCT",
    OL = "Losses",
    OW = "Wins",
    BA = "Bowl Appearance?" 
    ) |> 
   tab_header(
    title = "How the new additions compare",
    subtitle = "Here is the current state of the Mountain West Conference at the end of the 2025-26 season."
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("**By:** Jason Balderaz  |  **Source:** Sports-Reference.com")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
   ) |> 
   opt_row_striping() |>
    opt_table_lines("none") |>
    fmt_percent(
    columns = c(OPct, CPct),
    decimals = 1
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "#041E42"),
      cell_text(color = "white")
      ),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = School == "UTEP")
  ) |> 
    tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "#BA0C2F"),
      cell_text(color = "white")
      ),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = School == "Northern Illinois")
  )
How the new additions compare
Here is the current state of the Mountain West Conference at the end of the 2025-26 season.
School Conf Wins Losses PCT CW CL CPct SRS SOS Bowl Appearance?
Boise State MWC 9 5 64.3% 6 2 75.0% 3.69 -1.10 Y
Nevada-Las Vegas MWC 10 4 71.4% 6 2 75.0% -0.12 -6.05 Y
New Mexico MWC 9 4 69.2% 6 2 75.0% 0.67 -4.64 Y
San Diego State MWC 9 4 69.2% 6 2 75.0% 3.48 -5.06 Y
Fresno State MWC 9 4 69.2% 5 3 62.5% -0.82 -6.36 Y
Hawaii MWC 9 4 69.2% 5 3 62.5% -0.17 -5.71 Y
Utah State MWC 6 7 46.2% 4 4 50.0% -2.75 -2.06 Y
Air Force MWC 4 8 33.3% 3 5 37.5% -6.35 -4.18 N
Wyoming MWC 4 8 33.3% 2 6 25.0% -10.09 -4.92 N
Nevada MWC 3 9 25.0% 2 6 25.0% -13.55 -3.96 N
San Jose State MWC 3 9 25.0% 2 6 25.0% -13.03 -3.78 N
Colorado State MWC 2 10 16.7% 1 7 12.5% -12.63 -0.71 N
Northern Illinois MAC 3 9 25.0% 2 6 25.0% -14.25 -7.08 N
UTEP CUSA 2 10 16.7% 1 7 12.5% -14.63 -7.21 N
By: Jason Balderaz | Source: Sports-Reference.com

Both UTEP and Northern Illinois did not have a successful season last season. If they had joined a year earlier, it would have ranked them both second to last and dead last in the conference in terms of SRS. Clearly, this is a situation of trying to freshen things up for both parties. It’s a move to stop the bleeding for the conference after the Pac-12 disaster. For UTEP and NIU, it will give them the chance to be in a new environment with new recruiting strategies. It will be interesting to see how North Dakota State fares in their first season in the FBS and if their success in the FCS will transfer over.

The Mountain West is undeniably weaker off now with those teams leaving and not adding much of value in return. As of right now we can only speculate what can become of the Mountain West and the Pac-12 as we enter this new era of college football.

item.title: The Colorado Avalanche are the most dominant team in hockey, but they have a major weakness
item.dc:creator: Jason Balderaz
item.description:
The Colorado Avalanche are having a historic season. They are the first team in the league to reach 50 wins and have already clinched the #1 seed in the Western Conference and have won the Presidents’ Trophy. But how dominant have they truly been?

There is no doubt that the Avalanche are the best team in the NHL right now. They lead the league in most goals scored at 287, least goals against at 191 and lead the league in penalty kill percentage at 81.5%

That means Colorado has consistently had the #1 offense and #1 defense all season, and it’s thanks to their team dynamic.

The big three of Nathan MacKinnon, Cale Makar and Martin Nečas are all top-30 in the league in points, tied for the most players in the top-30 with the Edmonton Oilers.

There are many factors to the Avalanche’s success, so let’s take a look at an example of how successful they have been compared to their competition.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(gt)

teamstats <- read_csv("Team Stats as of April 7.csv")

avs <- teamstats |> filter(Team == "Colorado Avalanche")

ggplot() + geom_point(data=teamstats, aes(x=GF, y=GA, size=W), color="lightgrey") + 
  geom_point(data=avs, aes(x=GF, y=GA, size=W), color="#6F263D") +
  geom_text_repel(
    data=avs, 
    aes(x=GF, y=GA, label=Team, hjust = -2, vjust = -2)) +
  labs(
    x="Goals Scored", 
    y="Goals Against",
    size="Wins",
    title="Dominance at both ends of the rink", 
    subtitle="The Avs have the most goals scored and the least goals scored against them this season, and their wins reflect that", 
    caption="Source: NHL.com | By Jason Balderaz"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=9), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    )

Colorado’s goals for this season make up 3.73% of all goals made this season, which doesn’t seem like a lot, but it is the largest share among other NHL teams. This means Colorado’s offensive firepower is unmatched and continues to improve.

The defense has been just as dominant. As said earlier, the Avs lead the league in penalty kill percentage. Led by two-time Norris trophy winner, Cale Makar, alongside Devon Toews and Sam Malinski, but the real money makers are goalie duo, Scott Wedgewood and Mackenzie Blackwood, who together have earned the top spot in the league in goals against.

But with so much going right for Colorado, there is one glaring issue that has plagued them since last year: the power play.

Code 
ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=teamstats, aes(x=reorder(Team, `Net PP%`), weight=`Net PP%`), fill = "lightgray") + 
  geom_bar(data=avs, aes(x=reorder(Team, `Net PP%`), weight=`Net PP%`), fill= "#236192") +
  coord_flip() +
   labs(
    x="Team", 
    y="Power Play Percentage",
    title="The Avs achilles heel", 
    subtitle="Colorado is second to dead last in power play percentage, how could such a dominant team have such a weakness?", 
    caption="Source: NHL.com | By Jason Balderaz"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=9), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    )

In the 2025 playoffs, Colorado was eliminated in the first round by the Dallas Stars after seven games. The sore thumb in that scenario was that the Avs special teams unit was really, really bad, going 3-for-22 and 13.6% on the power play for the entire series. This directly resulted in power play coach Ray Bennett being fired.

However, it seems that the struggles never ended, even after the hiring of new assistant coach Dave Hakstol. As of April 7, 2026, the Avalanche sit 31st in the NHL for power play success rate.

So, with as historic of a season they are having, will their power play woes really matter? Obviously, it was a heavy factor last season, but how does it stack up against previous Lord Stanley winners?

Code 
stanleywinners <- read_csv("stanleywinners.csv")

stanleywinners |>
  gt() |> 
   tab_header(
    title = "Low power play percentage doesn't equal certain doom",
    subtitle = "Here are the last 10 Stanley Cup Champions and how their net power play faired in their golden year"
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("**By:** Jason Balderaz  |  **Source:** NHL.com")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
   ) |> 
   opt_row_striping() |> 
  tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "#6F263D"),
      cell_text(color = "white")
      ),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = Team == "Colorado Avalanche")
  ) |> 
    tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "#c8102E"),
      cell_text(color = "white")
      ),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = Team == "Florida Panthers" & Season == "2023-24")
  )
Low power play percentage doesn't equal certain doom
Here are the last 10 Stanley Cup Champions and how their net power play faired in their golden year
Team Season Net PP% League Rank
Florida Panthers 2024-25 19.3 18
Florida Panthers 2023-24 17.1 19
Vegas Golden Knights 2022-23 18.4 14
Colorado Avalanche 2021-22 22.2 6
Tampa Bay Lightning 2020-21 20.6 8
Tampa Bay Lightning 2019-20 19.8 8
St. Louis Blues 2018-19 18.1 10
Washington Capitals 2017-18 19.3 8
Pittsburgh Penguins 2016-17 20.4 5
Pittsburgh Penguins 2015-16 16.5 12
By: Jason Balderaz | Source: NHL.com

It’s not uncommon for Stanley Cup winners to have a low power play percentage. In recent years, teams haven’t even cracked the top 10 in net power play percentage. The first Florida Panthers chip had their power play at 17.1%, good enough for 19th in the league. It also goes to show how good that 2022 Colorado team was, having the highest net PP% out of the last 10 winners.

Since after the Olympic break, the Avalanche’s power play has actually improved, spiking to 18.8% since play has resumed. New additions, such as Nazem Kadri, have added extra firepower to the PP 1 unit, and the future still looks bright for the Avs.
